content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert H. Pittman 
Burke, Williams & Sorensen, LLP 
1901 Harrison Street, Suite 900 
Oakland, CA 94612-350 I 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-14-039 
Dear Mr. Pittman: 
March 19, 2014 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Cupertino Mayor Gilbert 
Wong, regarding his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
(the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby 
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that all advice is based only on the provisions of the Act. 
We offer no opinion on the application of other laws that may apply, such as common law 
conflict-of-interest rules. 
QUESTIONS 
(1) May the City Council decisions on which properties to include in the housing 
inventory list of the Housing Element and related amendments to the General Plan be segmented 
under the provisions of the Act to allow Mayor Wong to participate in certain portions of those 
determinations, as well as the adoption of the General Plan and Housing Element updates as a 
whole? 
(2) Assuming the decision of which properties to include on the housing inventory list 
can be segmented, may Mayor Wong participate in preliminary discussions and consideration of 
the Housing Element and General Plan Amendments (such as study sessions, environmental 
scoping sessions, etc.) provided all discussion of the decisions impacting his interests are 
segmented from those discussions? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All s1a1utory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulalory references are 10 Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 2 
( 1) If the Housing Element and General Plan decisions can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the mayor has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest, the mayor may participate in these other unrelated decisions so 
long as: 
• The inclusion of property belonging to Mayor Wong's father, Allen Wong ("Mr. 
Wong"), in the housing inventory list of the Housing Element and associated 
amendments to the General Plan must be determined first and a final decision reached by 
the agency without the mayor's participation. 
• The subsequent decisions will not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, 
the decision from which the mayor was disqualified. 
• The mayor does not have a conflict of interest in the subsequent decisions based on other 
interests as specified in Section 87103. 
Moreover, Regulation 18709(c) provides that once all the separate decisions related to a 
general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may 
participate in the final vote to adopt, reject, or amend the General Plan. 
(2) The segmentation rule requires that decisions in which the mayor has an interest be 
determined first. In other words, until Mr. Wong's applications are determined definitively, the 
mayor may not participate in any of the General Plan or Housing Element decisions, including 
preliminary discussions, study sessions, and environmental scoping sessions. 
Moreover, when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a 
conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: ( 1) immediately 
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as 
well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting 
(2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or 
vote on the item. 
FACTS 
The City of Cupertino is a general law city located in Santa Clara County. The City 
encompasses an area of 11.3 square miles and has a current estimated population of 58,302. 
Mayor Wong was elected to the Cupertino City Council on November 6, 2007, and was re­
elected to a second term on November 8, 2011. Your firm serves as special counsel to the City 
of Cupertino (the "City") in regard to the City's consideration and adoption of its current 
Housing Element Update and a comprehensive General Plan Amendment. 
The City is in the process of amending its General Plan and preparing its statutorily 
mandated Housing Element Update for the next planning cycle in the following respects: 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 3 
• The amendments to the General Plan include, among other things, changes to the Land 
Use Element to revise existing density and height restrictions in certain major corridors. 
In addition, the amendments can affect which properties are included on the housing 
inventory list discussed below. 
• The Housing Elemelll includes the identification of suitable sites for future residential 
development to be placed on the available housing inventory list and the adoption of 
citywide goals and policies to encourage the development of housing affordable to all 
segments of the community. Further, you stated that the process to determine which 
properties are included on the inventory list is competitive -- a decision to include one 
property could affect the decision to include (or, in fact, preclude the inclusion of) other 
properties on this list. The City has identified more than 50 parcels for potential 
inclusion on the inventory list. These 50 potential sites offer future residential 
development potential well in excess of the number of parcels the City needs to satisfy its 
Regional Housing Needs Allocation for the current planning cycle. 
The General Plan and Housing Element updates are on a parallel track and will be 
environmentally reviewed in a single environmental document. 
Mr. Wong, the mayor's father, is the sole owner and president of Wong Young 
Development Corp. ("WDC") headquartered in San Jose. Mr. Wong manages and controls 
WDC, which employs both Mayor Wong and his wife. 
Separate from Mr. Wong's interest in WDC, Mr. Wong owns the following two parcels 
of land in the City of Cupertino and has submitted an application requesting that the Property be 
placed on the housing inventory list: 
• The parcel at 10931 Maxine Avenue is approximately 1.53 acres in size and is currently 
designated for quasi-public use in the General Plan. This property would require that the 
City change the General Plan land use designation and rezone the parcel to be placed on 
the housing inventory list. 
• The parcel at 10921 Maxine A venue is 10,000 square feet in size and is currently zoned 
for single-family residential uses. 
Mayor Wong's personal residence is not located within 500 feet of any of the sites 
under consideration for the housing inventory list in the Housing Element; nor is it located 
within 500 feet of any of the study areas affected by the General Plan. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 4 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 
l 8700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. 
You have identified Mayor Wong as a public official who is being called upon to make 
governmental decisions, and you have delineated the three interests that may result in a conflict 
of interest -- his home, his employer (WDC) as a source of income, 2 and the sole owner of his 
employer (Mr. Wong). 3 Thus, we do not discuss the first three steps of the standard analysis. 4 
Step Four: Are the official's interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision? 
Real Property 
Under Regulation 18704.2(b)(3) real property in which a public official has an interest is 
considered indirectly involved if the decision solely concerns the adoption or amendment of a 
general plan and all of the following apply: 
"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise 
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not qualify under this subdivision if the 
decision is initiated by the public official, by a person that is an economic interest 
of the public official, or by a person representing either the public official or an 
economic interest of the public official. 
(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public 
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objectives. 
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting, 
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, land use 
ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent. 
2 WDC is also a potentially disqualifying interest of the Mayor since he holds a business position at WDC. 
3 Regulation I 8703.3(a){3) provides: "In addition 10 having an economic interest in any business entity 
from which the official has received income of five hundred ($500) or more within 12 months prior to the lime when 
the relevant governmental decision is made, the official has a source.of-income economic interest in all of the 
following: (A) Any individual owning a 50 percent or greater interest in that business entity. (B) Any individual, 
regardless of the extent of the individual's ownership interest in that entity, who has the power lo direct or cause the 
direction of the management and policies of the business entity." 
c When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a 
decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (I) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally 
identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation 
18702.5{b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the 
discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, and speaking as a member of 
the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.S(c) and 18702.S{d) apply. 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 5 
(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or 
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or 
parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the decision 
is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted on a map or 
diagram offered in connection with the decision, provided that the map or 
diagram depicts all parcels located within the agency's jurisdiction and economic 
interests of the official are not singled out. 151 
(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or 
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, zoning designation, 
zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent." 
(Emphasis added.) 
You stated that Mayor Wong's personal residence is not located within 500 feet of any of 
the sites under consideration for the housing inventory list in the Housing Element. You also 
stated that you believed the criterion in the regulation was met and that the exception applied. 
Assuming this is the case, the exception would apply and the property would be 
considered indirectly involved in the decision. 
Sources of Income: 
Regulation 18704.l (a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly 
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of income 
either directly or by an agent: 
"( 1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a 
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or 
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." 
5 We have advised that "so long as the discussion docs not involve an 'identifiable parcel or parcels' (i.e. 
the discussion does not specifically concern any of the properties with boundaries located within 500 feet of the real 
property economic interest of one of the councilmembers), they may participate in the public hearing phase. 
However, if it does involve an "identifiable parcel or parcels," the exception would not be applicable, and the real 
property of any official located within 500 feet of the "identifiable parcel or parcels" would still be directly involved 
in that part of the decision and that official would have to follow the procedures set forth in Regulation 18702.5." 
(Barker Advice Letter, No. 1-08-170.) 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 6 
Sources of income and business entities that are not directly involved in governmental 
decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 
18704(a) and 18704.l(b).) 
According to your facts, WDC is not an applicant or named party in any aspect of the 
decisions in question. However, Mr. Wong has requested the City include the Property into the 
housing inventory list of the Housing Element. Thus, Mr. Wong is an applicant with respect to 
the Housing Element decision and associated General Plan amendments. 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
official's interests? 
Having identified the interests involved, and determined whether each interest is directly 
or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality standard 
applicable to each interest and whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect will meet the applicable standard of materiality. For a material financial effect to be 
foreseeable on an official's interest, it need not be certain or even substantially likely that it will 
happen. However, the financial effect must be more than a mere possibility. (Regulation 
18706(a); /11 re Thomer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Real Property 
A financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be 
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its 
financial effect, and the nature of the real property, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property. 
You stated that you were aware of no facts concerning decisions that suggest they would 
have an indirect material financial effect on Mayor Wong's real property; therefore, we do not 
further analyze potential indirect effects on his property. 
Sources of l11come 
WDC: As noted above, WDC would be indirectly involved in the decision. Therefore, 
according to Regulation 18705.1, a financial effect would be material if: 
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in 
the value of the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of 
$20,000 or more; or, 
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring 
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a 
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or, 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 7 
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the 
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." 
You have not provided any facts suggesting that there will be any financial effect on 
WDC. However, if WDC will be affected by the decision to the applicable thresholds in 
Regulation 18705.1, the effect would be material. 
Mr. Wong: Mr. Wong is subject to a stricter test since he is an applicant in connection 
with the decisions and therefore directly involved. Regulation 18705.3 provides that any 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, 
and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material. 
Therefore, absent an exception, the mayor may not participate in the identification of suitable 
sites for future residential development to be placed on the available housing inventory list and 
associated General Plan amendments. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions 
Having analyzed the facts surrounding the decisions, you have concluded that neither of 
the exceptions apply. 
Segmentation 
As you note, under certain circumstances, complex decisions may be divided into 
separate decisions, thus enabling an official who has a disqualifying conflict of interest in one 
component of the decision to participate as to other components in which the official has no 
financial interest. (Regulation 18709; Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. 1-90-542; Huffaker Advice 
Letter, No. A-86-343.) 
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
(I) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken 
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision 
in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 8 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered 
first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified 
official's participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been 
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening 
of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified. 
(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are .. inextricably 
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions 
that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the official has a disqualifying 
conflict of interest. For example, you asked whether segmentation of the housing inventory list 
of the Housing Element could be performed by region. However, according to your facts, the 
process to determine which properties are included on the inventory list citywide is competitive 
- a decision to include one property could affect the decision to include (or, in fact, preclude the 
inclusion of) other properties on this list. Therefore, all of the decisions concerning the housing 
inventory list are inextricably interrelated and cannot be segmented. 
Finally, under the guidelines of Regulation 18709, the inclusion of Mr. Wong's Property 
in the housing inventory list of the Housing Element and associated amendments to the General 
Plan must be voted on first and a final decision reached by the agency without the mayor's 
participation. Once the entire housing inventory list is finalized and associated General Plan 
amendments determined, the mayor may participate in subsequent decisions so long as: 
• The subsequent decisions will not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, 
the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
• The official does not have a conflict of interest in the subsequent decisions based on other 
interests as specified in Section 87103. 
Regulation 18709(c) provides an additional exception that provides that once all the 
separate decisions related to a general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, 
the public official may participate in the final vote to adopt, reject, or amend the general plan. 


File No. A-14-039 
Page No. 9 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
eneral Counsel 
ace 
t eneral Counsel

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a  
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation

employer (Mr. Wong). 3 Thus, we do not discuss the first three steps of the standard analysis. 4

of the public official, or by a person representing either the public official or an 
economic interest of the public official. 
(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public 
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objectives. 
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting, 
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, land use 
ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent. 
2 WDC is also a potentially disqualifying interest of the Mayor since he holds a business position at WDC. 
3 Regulation I 8703.3(a){3) provides: "In addition 10 having an economic interest in any business entity 
from which the official has received income of five hundred ($500) or more within 12 months prior to the lime when

direction of the management and policies of the business entity."

discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, and speaking as a member of

(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or 
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or 
parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the decision 
is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted on a map or

Assuming this is the case, the exception would apply and the property would be

Sources of Income: 
Regulation 18704.l (a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly 
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of income

"( 1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing

revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person."

the value of the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of

or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a

(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the 
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." 
You have not provided any facts suggesting that there will be any financial effect on 
WDC. However, if WDC will be affected by the decision to the applicable thresholds in 
Regulation 18705.1, the effect would be material. 
Mr. Wong: Mr. Wong is subject to a stricter test since he is an applicant in connection 
with the decisions and therefore directly involved. Regulation 18705.3 provides that any 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, 
and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material. 
Therefore, absent an exception, the mayor may not participate in the identification of suitable 
sites for future residential development to be placed on the available housing inventory list and 
associated General Plan amendments. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions 
Having analyzed the facts surrounding the decisions, you have concluded that neither of 
the exceptions apply. 
Segmentation 
As you note, under certain circumstances, complex decisions may be divided into 
separate decisions, thus enabling an official who has a disqualifying conflict of interest in one 
component of the decision to participate as to other components in which the official has no 
financial interest. (Regulation 18709; Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. 1-90-542; Huffaker Advice 
Letter, No. A-86-343.) 
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
(I) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken 
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision 
in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions;  
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered 
first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified 
official's participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been 
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening 
of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified. 
(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are .. inextricably 
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions 
that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the official has a disqualifying 
conflict of interest. For example, you asked whether segmentation of the housing inventory list 
of the Housing Element could be performed by region. However, according to your facts, the 
process to determine which properties are included on the inventory list citywide is competitive 
- a decision to include one property could affect the decision to include (or, in fact, preclude the 
inclusion of) other properties on this list. Therefore, all of the decisions concerning the housing 
inventory list are inextricably interrelated and cannot be segmented. 
Finally, under the guidelines of Regulation 18709, the inclusion of Mr. Wong's Property 
in the housing inventory list of the Housing Element and associated amendments to the General 
Plan must be voted on first and a final decision reached by the agency without the mayor's 
participation. Once the entire housing inventory list is finalized and associated General Plan 
amendments determined, the mayor may participate in subsequent decisions so long as: 
• The subsequent decisions will not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, 
the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
• The official does not have a conflict of interest in the subsequent decisions based on other 
interests as specified in Section 87103. 
Regulation 18709(c) provides an additional exception that provides that once all the 
separate decisions related to a general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, 
the public official may participate in the final vote to adopt, reject, or amend the general plan.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) May the City Council decisions on which properties to include in the housing 
inventory list of the Housing Element and related amendments to the General Plan be segmented 
under the provisions of the Act to allow Mayor Wong to participate in certain portions of those 
determinations, as well as the adoption of the General Plan and Housing Element updates as a 
whole? 
(2) Assuming the decision of which properties to include on the housing inventory list 
can be segmented, may Mayor Wong participate in preliminary discussions and consideration of 
the Housing Element and General Plan Amendments (such as study sessions, environmental 
scoping sessions, etc.) provided all discussion of the decisions impacting his interests are 
segmented from those discussions?All 
regulalory references are 10 Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

CONCLUSION: ( 1) If the Housing Element and General Plan decisions can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the mayor has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest, the mayor may participate in these other unrelated decisions so 
long as: 
• The inclusion of property belonging to Mayor Wong's father, Allen Wong ("Mr. 
Wong"), in the housing inventory list of the Housing Element and associated 
amendments to the General Plan must be determined first and a final decision reached by 
the agency without the mayor's participation. 
• The subsequent decisions will not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, 
the decision from which the mayor was disqualified. 
• The mayor does not have a conflict of interest in the subsequent decisions based on other 
interests as specified in Section 87103. 
Moreover, Regulation 18709(c) provides that once all the separate decisions related to a 
general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may 
participate in the final vote to adopt, reject, or amend the General Plan. 
(2) The segmentation rule requires that decisions in which the mayor has an interest be 
determined first. In other words, until Mr. Wong's applications are determined definitively, the 
mayor may not participate in any of the General Plan or Housing Element decisions, including 
preliminary discussions, study sessions, and environmental scoping sessions. 
Moreover, when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a 
conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: ( 1) immediately 
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as 
well as details of the interest as discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting 
(2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or 
vote on the item.

The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that all advice is based only on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application of other laws that may apply, such as common law conflict-of-interest rules. (2) Assuming the decision of which properties to include on the housing inventory list can be segmented, may Mayor Wong participate in preliminary discussions and consideration of the Housing Element and General Plan Amendments (such as study sessions, environmental scoping sessions, etc.) provided all discussion of the decisions impacting his interests are segmented from those discussions? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All s1a1utory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulalory references are 10 Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
